debug_info.goal:
α : Type u_1
C : Category α
X Y : α
f : Hom X Y
⊢ @CategoryInContext.Category.split_epi α C X Y f ↔ ∀ (c : α), Function.Surjective fun g => comp f g

failed_attempts:
split epi, then for all c, the map g ↦ f ≫ g is surjective.
    intro h_split
    have h : ∀ c : α, Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y c => f ≫ g) := by
      intro c
      have h_surj : Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y c => f ≫ g) := by
        -- Use the fact that f is a split epi to find a surjection.
        have h1 : split_epi f := h_split
        -- Obtain the retraction g such that f ≫ g = id X.
        obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h1
        -- For any h : Hom Y c, we can find a preimage by composing with g.
        intro h2
        use h2 ≫ g
        -- Show that (h2 ≫ g) ≫ f = h2.
        have h3 : (h2 ≫ g) ≫ f = h2 := by
          calc
            (h2 ≫ g) ≫ f = h2 ≫ (g ≫ f) := by rw [assoc]
            _ = h2 ≫ (id X) := by rw [hg]
            _ = h2 := by simp [id_comp]
        -- Simplify the expression to show that (h2 ≫ g) ≫ f = h2.
        simp_all [comp_assoc]
        <;>
        aesop
      exact h_surj
    exact h
  · -- Prove the reverse direction: if for all c, the map g ↦ f ≫ g is surjective, then f is a split epi.
    intro h_surj
    -- Use the surjectivity condition to find a retraction.
    have h_split : split_epi f := by
      -- Use the surjectivity at c = X to find a retraction.
      have h1 : Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y X => f ≫ g) := h_surj X
      -- Obtain a preimage of id X under the map g ↦ f ≫ g.
      obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h1 (id X)
      -- Show that f ≫ g = id X.
      have h2 : f ≫ g = id X := by
        simpa [id_comp] using hg
      -- Conclude that f is a split epi with retraction g.
      exact ⟨g, h2⟩
    exact h_split

simp [one_comp]

    -- Use c = X and h = id X to find a right inverse g : Hom Y X

    -- Verify that g is indeed a right inverse
    refine' ⟨g, _⟩
    simpa [one_comp] using hg

      simpa [isRetraction] using hg
    obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h₁

    have h₂ : ∃ (h : Hom Y c), f ≫ h = z := by

        _ = id X ≫ z := by rw [hg]

  · -- Prove the backward direction: (∀ c : α, Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y c => f ≫ g)) → split_epi f

    obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h₁

split_epi f ↔ ∃ g : Hom Y X, f ≫ g = id X := by
    constructor <;> intro h
    · -- Prove the forward direction: if split_epi f, then ∃ g : Hom Y X, f ≫ g = id X
      obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h
      exact ⟨g, hg⟩
    · -- Prove the reverse direction: if ∃ g : Hom Y X, f ≫ g = id X, then split_epi f
      obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h
      exact ⟨g, hg⟩
  have h₂ : (∀ c : α, Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y c => f ≫ g)) ↔ ∃ g : Hom Y X, f ≫ g = id X := by
    constructor
    · -- Prove the forward direction: if ∀ c, surjective, then ∃ g : Hom Y X, f ≫ g = id X
      intro h
      -- Use X as the codomain to get a g such that f ≫ g = id X
      have h₃ : Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y X => f ≫ g) := h X
      -- Since the function is surjective, there exists a g such that f ≫ g = id X
      have h₄ : (id X : Hom X X) ∈ Set.range (fun g : Hom Y X => f ≫ g) := by
        apply h₃
        <;> simp [Hom.ext_iff]
      -- Obtain g from the surjectivity condition
      obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h₄
      refine' ⟨g, _⟩
      -- Simplify to get f ≫ g = id X
      simpa using hg
    · -- Prove the reverse direction: if ∃ g : Hom Y X, f ≫ g = id X, then ∀ c, surjective
      intro h
      obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h
      intro c
      -- Show that for any c, the function g ↦ f ≫ g is surjective
      have h₃ : Function.Surjective (fun (g : Hom Y c) => f ≫ g) := by
        intro p
        -- For any p : Hom X c, we need to find g : Hom Y c such that f ≫ g = p
        have h₄ : f ≫ (g ≫ p) = p := by
          calc
            f ≫ (g ≫ p) = (f ≫ g) ≫ p := by rw [assoc]
            _ = id X ≫ p := by rw [hg]
            _ = p := by simp [id_comp]
        -- Use g ≫ p as the required g
        refine' ⟨g ≫ p, _⟩
        simpa using h₄
      exact h₃
  -- Combine h₁ and h₂ to get the final result
  rw [h₁]
  rw [h₂]

      intro h

      rcases h with ⟨g, hg⟩
      -- To show that g ↦ f ≫ g is surjective, take any h : Hom Y c and find a preimage.
      intro h_target
      -- Use the composition h_target ∘ g as the preimage.
      use h_target ≫ g
      -- Simplify the composition (h_target ≫ g) ≫ f to show it equals h_target.
      calc
        f ≫ (h_target ≫ g) = (f ≫ g) ≫ h_target := by rw [assoc]
        _ = id X ≫ h_target := by rw [hg]
        _ = h_target := by simp [id_comp]
    · -- Prove the reverse direction: if for every c, the map g ↦ f ≫ g is surjective, then f is a split epi.
      intro h
      -- Use the surjectivity condition with c = X to find a right inverse.
      have h₁ : Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y X => f ≫ g) := h X
      -- Since the map is surjective, there exists a g : Hom Y X such that f ≫ g = id X.
      have h₂ : ∃ (g : Hom Y X), f ≫ g = id X := by
        -- Apply the surjectivity to id X.
        have h₃ : ∃ (g : Hom Y X), f ≫ g = id X := by
          -- Use the fact that id X is in the codomain of the map.
          have h₄ : (id X : Hom X X) ∈ Set.range (fun g : Hom Y X => f ≫ g) := by
            apply h₁
          -- Extract the preimage g from the range.
          rcases h₄ with ⟨g, hg⟩
          refine' ⟨g, _⟩
          -- Simplify the equation to show f ≫ g = id X.
          simpa using hg
        exact h₃
      -- Obtain the right inverse g from the existence statement.
      rcases h₂ with ⟨g, hg⟩
      -- Conclude that f is a split epi by providing the right inverse g.
      exact ⟨g, hg⟩
  -- Use the intermediate result to complete the proof.
  exact h_goal

simp_all [comp_assoc]
    <;> try simp_all [hg]
    <;> try simp_all [comp_assoc]
  · intro h
    have h' : ∃ (g : Hom Y X), f ≫ g = id X := by
      have h₁ := h X
      have h₂ : Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y X => f ≫ g) := h₁
      have h₃ : ∃ (g : Hom Y X), f ≫ g = id X := by

        have h₅ : ∃ (g : Hom Y X), f ≫ g = id X := by

split epi, then for all c, (fun g => f ≫ g) is surjective.
    intro h
    rcases h with ⟨g, hg⟩
    intro c
    intro g₁
    -- For any g₁ : Hom Y c, we need to find g₂ : Hom X c such that f ≫ g₂ = g₁.
    -- Since f is a split epi, we can use the retraction g to construct g₂.
    have h₂ : f ≫ g = id X := by
      have h₃ := hg
      simpa [id] using h₃
    -- Use g₁ ≫ g as g₂ and check that f ≫ (g₁ ≫ g) = g₁.
    use g₁ ≫ g
    calc
      f ≫ (g₁ ≫ g) = (f ≫ g₁) ≫ g := by rw [comp_assoc]
      _ = g₁ := by
        have h₄ : f ≫ g = id X := by simpa [id] using hg
        have h₅ : (f ≫ g₁) ≫ g = g₁ := by
          calc
            (f ≫ g₁) ≫ g = f ≫ (g₁ ≫ g) := by rw [comp_assoc]
            _ = (f ≫ g) ≫ g₁ := by
              rw [show (g₁ ≫ g : Hom X X) = (g₁ ≫ g : Hom X X) by rfl]
              simp_all [comp_assoc]
              <;>
              try simp_all [comp_assoc]
            _ = id X ≫ g₁ := by rw [hg]
            _ = g₁ := by simp [id]
        simp_all [comp_assoc]
        <;>
        try simp_all [comp_assoc]
  · -- Prove the reverse direction: if for all c, (fun g => f ≫ g) is surjective, then f is a split epi.
    intro h
    -- For X itself, since (fun g => f ≫ g) is surjective, there exists a g : Hom Y X such that f ≫ g = id X.
    have h₂ : Function.Surjective (fun (g : Hom Y X) => f ≫ g) := h X
    -- Therefore, f is a split epi.
    have h₃ : ∃ (g : Hom Y X), f ≫ g = id X := by
      have h₄ : id X ∈ Set.range (fun (g : Hom Y X) => f ≫ g) := by
        have h₅ : Function.Surjective (fun (g : Hom Y X) => f ≫ g) := h X
        have h₆ : id X ∈ Set.range (fun (g : Hom Y X) => f ≫ g) := by
          obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h₅ (id X)
          refine' ⟨g, _⟩
          simp_all
        exact h₆
      obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h₄
      refine' ⟨g, _⟩
      simp_all
    obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h₃
    refine' ⟨g, _⟩
    -- Check that g is indeed a retraction for f.
    have h₅ : f ≫ g = id X := hg
    simpa [id] using h₅

split epi, then for all c, (fun g => f ≫ g) is surjective.
    intro h
    have h₁ : ∃ (g : Hom Y X), f ≫ g = id X := by
      -- Extract the retraction from the definition of split_eiso.
      rcases h with ⟨g, hg⟩
      refine' ⟨g, _⟩
      <;> simpa using hg
    intro c
    -- Use the retraction to show surjectivity.
    have h₂ : Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y c => f ≫ g) := by
      -- Take any h : Hom X c, we need to find g : Hom Y c such that f ≫ g = h.
      intro h
      -- Obtain the retraction g : Hom Y X.
      rcases h₁ with ⟨g, hg⟩
      -- Define g' := g ≫ h.
      use g ≫ h
      -- Show that f ≫ (g ≫ h) = h.
      calc
        f ≫ (g ≫ h) = (f ≫ g) ≫ h := by rw [comp_assoc]
        _ = id X ≫ h := by rw [hg]
        _ = h := by simp [id_comp]
    exact h₂
  · -- Prove the backward direction: if for all c, (fun g => f ≫ g) is surjective, then f is split epi.
    intro h
    -- Take c := X, the surjectivity condition gives us a retraction.
    have h₁ : Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y X => f ≫ g) := h X
    -- There exists a g : Hom Y X such that f ≫ g = id X.
    have h₂ : ∃ (g : Hom Y X), f ≫ g = id X := by
      -- Use the surjectivity to find such a g.
      have h₃ : (id X : Hom X X) ∈ Set.range (fun g : Hom Y X => f ≫ g) := by
        apply h₁
        <;> simp
      -- Extract the g from the range.
      rcases h₃ with ⟨g, hg⟩
      refine' ⟨g, _⟩
      <;> simp_all
    -- Therefore, f is a split epi.
    rcases h₂ with ⟨g, hg⟩
    refine' ⟨g, _⟩
    <;> simp_all

split_epi f → ∀ c : α, Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y c => f ≫ g)
    intro h
    calc
      (∀ c : α, Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y c => f ≫ g)) = (∀ c : α, Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y c => f ≫ g)) := rfl
      _ := by
        intro c
        -- Use the fact that f is a split epimorphism to show the surjectivity
        have h₁ : ∃ (g : Hom Y X), IsRetraction f g := h
        rcases h₁ with ⟨g, hg⟩
        -- Use the retraction to show surjectivity
        intro z
        use g ≫ z
        <;> simp [hg, comp_assoc]
        <;>
        (try simp_all [IsRetraction]) <;>
        (try simp_all [comp_assoc]) <;>
        (try simp_all [comp_id]) <;>
        (try simp_all [id_comp]) <;>
        (try simp_all [Function.Bijective]) <;>
        (try simp_all [Function.Injective]) <;>
        (try simp_all [Function.Surjective])
        <;>
        aesop
  · -- Prove the reverse direction: (∀ c : α, Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y c => f ≫ g)) → split_epi f
    intro h
    -- Use the surjectivity condition to construct a right inverse
    have h₁ : ∃ (g : Hom Y X), f ≫ g = id X := by
      -- Use the surjectivity at c = X
      have h₂ : Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y X => f ≫ g) := h X
      -- Find a g such that f ≫ g = id X
      have h₃ : ∃ (g : Hom Y X), f ≫ g = id X := by
        have h₄ : id X ∈ Set.range (fun g : Hom Y X => f ≫ g) := by
          -- id X is in the range of (fun g : Hom Y X => f ≫ g)
          have h₅ : Function.Surjective (fun g : Hom Y X => f ≫ g) := h₂
          -- Since the function is surjective, id X is in the range
          exact h₅ (id X)
        -- Obtain the g such that f ≫ g = id X
        rcases h₄ with ⟨g, hg⟩
        refine' ⟨g, _⟩
        simpa using hg
      -- Obtain the g such that f ≫ g = id X
      exact h₃
    -- Obtain the g such that f ≫ g = id X
    rcases h₁ with ⟨g, hg⟩
    -- Show that f is a split epimorphism
    refine' ⟨g, _⟩
    -- Verify that g is a retraction of f
    <;> simp_all [IsRetraction]
    <;>
    (try simp_all [comp_assoc]) <;>
    (try simp_all [comp_id]) <;>
    (try simp_all [id_comp]) <;>
    (try simp_all [Function.Bijective]) <;>
    (try simp_all [Function.Injective]) <;>
    (try simp_all [Function.Surjective])
    <;>
    aesop
